unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 3: Life in the Wild! In this unit, you will explore the natural world through the lens of East African ecosystems. You will discover how animals, plants, and their environments are connected in amazing ways. Every living thing depends on something else to survive, and understanding these connections helps us take better care of the world around us.
unit.learningPath: Preview the goals and vocabulary. Read and annotate the source texts. Discuss evidence and language choices. Apply the grammar focus. Draft and revise two to three paragraphs. Present, reflect and complete the quiz.

visual.alt: Learners investigating ecosystems and evidence from the natural world

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Something that can cause harm or danger.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The dark clouds are a threat of a coming storm.
practiceSentences: The dark clouds are a threat of a coming storm.
The approaching drought was a serious threat to every crop in the valley.
Is losing their grazing land the biggest threat facing these wild herds?
"That lion poses no threat to us here," the ranger reassured the group.
I realised that ignoring a small threat can let it grow into real danger.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - h - r - e - a - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'threat', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Dangerous and not safe, often because it is not what it seems.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The path through the mountains was treacherous after the rains.
practiceSentences: The path through the mountains was treacherous after the rains.
The treacherous swamp swallowed footprints, hiding the safe path from careless travellers.
Why did the guide call the quiet-looking marsh so treacherous?
"These treacherous cliffs crumble easily," the ranger warned the young explorers.
I stepped carefully across the treacherous rocks slick with morning dew.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - r - e - a - c - h - e - r - o - u - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'treacherous', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A leader who has complete power and makes all the rules without asking others.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The dictator did not allow people to speak freely or choose their leaders.
practiceSentences: The dictator did not allow people to speak freely or choose their leaders.
The cruel dictator seized the villagers' land and silenced anyone who complained.
How can ordinary people resist a dictator who controls the army and the courts?
"A dictator rules by fear," the elder told the gathered children quietly.
I felt hopeful reading how the people finally removed the unjust dictator.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - i - c - t - a - t - o - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'dictator', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To treat someone in a cruel or unfair way, making them feel weak or powerless.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The soldiers tried to oppress the villagers by taking away their rights.
practiceSentences: The soldiers tried to oppress the villagers by taking away their rights.
The soldiers tried to oppress the farmers by seizing their harvest each season.
Why do the powerful so often oppress those who cannot easily fight back?
"They will not oppress us in silence," the elder declared before the crowd.
I admired the villagers who refused to let the officials oppress their community.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: o - p - p - r - e - s - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'oppress', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A difficult situation that causes suffering or trouble.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: After the drought, many families faced hardship because they lost their crops.
practiceSentences: After the drought, many families faced hardship because they lost their crops.
The long drought brought great hardship to families who depended on their crops.
How did the community help each other endure such terrible hardship?
"We have faced hardship before," Grandmother said, "and patience carried us through."
I learned that shared hardship can bring neighbours closer than easy, comfortable times.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - a - r - d - s - h - i - p
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'hardship', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To treat someone badly or unfairly because of their beliefs or identity.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: It is wrong to persecute people just because they are different.
practiceSentences: It is wrong to persecute people just because they are different.
It is deeply unjust to persecute anyone simply because of their beliefs.
Why would powerful rulers persecute the very people they promised to serve?
"They cannot persecute us for gathering," the elder said with quiet courage.
I felt sadness reading how the soldiers began to persecute the peaceful families.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - e - r - s - e - c - u - t - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'persecute', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To say that something is not allowed.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The soldiers tried to prohibit gatherings in the village.
practiceSentences: The soldiers tried to prohibit gatherings in the village.
The new rules prohibit any gathering of more than three villagers after sunset.
Why would the officials prohibit something as harmless as a wedding celebration?
"You cannot prohibit our prayers," the elder told the frowning soldier calmly.
I think it is wrong to prohibit children from playing safely in the park.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - r - o - h - i - b - i - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'prohibit', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To keep going through something difficult without giving up.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The villagers had to endure months of harsh rules and limited food.
practiceSentences: The villagers had to endure months of harsh rules and limited food.
The villagers had to endure many cold nights without enough food or firewood.
How long can anyone endure such unfair treatment before they finally speak out?
"We will endure this together," the elder promised the frightened families.
I try to endure difficult lessons because I know the effort will reward me.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - n - d - u - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'endure', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To say something loudly or officially.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The leader proclaimed that the village now belonged to a foreign power.
practiceSentences: The leader proclaimed that the village now belonged to a foreign power.
The commander proclaimed a curfew that stunned the peaceful, hardworking village.
When was independence finally proclaimed after so many years of struggle?
"The harvest is ours," the officer proclaimed loudly to the silent crowd.
I imagined how the villagers felt when the harsh new rules were proclaimed.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - r - o - c - l - a - i - m - e - d
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'proclaimed', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A ceremony to remember and say goodbye to someone who has died.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The whole community gathered at the funeral to honour the old farmer.
practiceSentences: The whole community gathered at the funeral to honour the old farmer.
The whole community walked quietly to the funeral of the beloved old farmer.
Who will lead the prayers at the funeral tomorrow morning?
"We must honour him at the funeral," Sami said, wiping away his tears.
I felt the deep respect that filled the crowd during my grandfather's funeral.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - u - n - e - r - a - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'funeral', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A feeling of being upset or annoyed because you cannot do something you want.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Sophia felt frustration when the soldiers banned their gathering.
practiceSentences: Sophia felt frustration when the soldiers banned their gathering.
Samira could not hide her frustration when the soldiers cancelled the market again.
How do you calm your frustration when a plan collapses at the last moment?
"Let go of your frustration," the elder advised, "and think clearly instead."
I felt real frustration after studying hard, yet the exam questions surprised me completely.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - r - u - s - t - r - a - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'frustration', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To say no to something or not accept it.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Mzee Ibrahim chose to refuse to hand over his harvest to the soldiers.
practiceSentences: Mzee Ibrahim chose to refuse to hand over his harvest to the soldiers.
Mzee Ibrahim chose to refuse the order to abandon his family's ancient land.
Would you refuse to obey a rule you believed was deeply unjust?
"I refuse to stay silent," the young teacher told the watching villagers.
I decided to refuse the unfair deal because it harmed my closest friend.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - e - f - u - s - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'refuse', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A system of managing a government or organisation with many rules and officials.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The soldiers set up a bureaucracy with papers and stamps to control the village.
practiceSentences: The soldiers set up a bureaucracy with papers and stamps to control the village.
The endless bureaucracy of stamps and forms delayed even the simplest requests.
Why does so much bureaucracy make helping ordinary people feel almost impossible?
"This bureaucracy is designed to exhaust us," the elder muttered wearily.
I grew frustrated with the bureaucracy that turned one letter into weeks of waiting.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - u - r - e - a - u - c - r - a - c - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'bureaucracy', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The group of people who manage a government, school, or organisation.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The administration centre was a stone building filled with rules.
practiceSentences: The administration centre was a stone building filled with rules.
The village administration met inside a cold stone building filled with strict rules.
Which part of the school administration decides how the timetable is arranged?
"The administration ignored our letters," the elder reported to the anxious crowd.
I learned that a fair administration listens carefully before making important decisions.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - d - m - i - n - i - s - t - r - a - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'administration', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who wants to end something, especially slavery.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Isaac was an abolitionist who fought slavery through song and secret messages.
practiceSentences: Isaac was an abolitionist who fought slavery through song and secret messages.
The brave abolitionist smuggled secret messages to help enslaved families escape.
Why did the abolitionist risk everything to end such a cruel practice?
"Isaac was a quiet abolitionist," the teacher said, "who fought through song."
I admire every abolitionist who believed that freedom belonged to all people.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - b - o - l - i - t - i - o - n - i - s - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'abolitionist', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Information, often false or misleading, used to influence how people think.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The soldiers called the school books propaganda and burned several of them.
practiceSentences: The soldiers called the school books propaganda and burned several of them.
The soldiers dismissed the schoolbooks as propaganda and burned several of them.
How can we recognise propaganda that pretends to be honest information?
"This poster is nothing but propaganda," the teacher warned her students.
I felt angry that harmful propaganda replaced the truthful lessons the children needed.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - r - o - p - a - g - a - n - d - a
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'propaganda', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The condition of not having enough food to eat, causing extreme hunger and weakness.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Without rain, the animals on the savannah face starvation.
practiceSentences: Without rain, the animals on the savannah face starvation.
Without rain, the weakened animals on the dry savannah faced slow starvation.
How can the villagers prevent starvation when their crops have completely failed?
"Starvation is closing in," the health worker warned the worried families.
I felt thankful for my meal after learning how starvation still threatens many.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - t - a - r - v - a - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'starvation', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A basic element found in all living things and in materials like coal and diamonds.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Plants take in carbon dioxide from the air to help them grow.
practiceSentences: Plants take in carbon dioxide from the air to help them grow.
Green plants absorb carbon dioxide from the air and use it to grow.
Why does burning too much fuel release harmful carbon into our atmosphere?
"Trees store carbon," the ranger explained, "which keeps our air cleaner."
I learned that reducing carbon emissions helps protect the fragile natural world.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - a - r - b - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'carbon', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A serious illness caused by bacteria that can spread through dirty water or food.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The health worker warned that typhoid was spreading because of unsafe water.
practiceSentences: The health worker warned that typhoid was spreading because of unsafe water.
The health worker warned that typhoid was spreading through the contaminated water.
How can a village stop typhoid before it makes many people seriously ill?
"Boil your water to avoid typhoid," the nurse advised the gathered families.
I realised how dangerous dirty water is after reading about the typhoid outbreak.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - y - p - h - o - i - d
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'typhoid', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Something that can be seen or noticed.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The migration of wildebeest is one of the most observable events in nature.
practiceSentences: The migration of wildebeest is one of the most observable events in nature.
The great migration of the wildebeest is one of nature's most observable wonders.
Which observable changes tell you that the dry season is finally ending?
"Record every observable detail," the ranger told the young wildlife trackers.
I noted the observable signs of rain gathering over the distant hills.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: o - b - s - e - r - v - a - b - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'observable', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person or thing that comes into a place and takes control without permission.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Invasive plant species can act as invaders in a habitat, pushing out native plants.
practiceSentences: Invasive plant species can act as invaders in a habitat, pushing out native plants.
An aggressive plant invader can push native flowers out of their natural home.
How does a single invader upset the balance of a whole habitat?
"This weed is an invader," the ranger explained, pulling it from the soil.
I learned that even a small invader can slowly overwhelm an entire ecosystem.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: i - n - v - a - d - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'invader', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A deep red colour.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The sky turned crimson as the sun set behind the mountains.
practiceSentences: The sky turned crimson as the sun set behind the mountains.
The evening sky turned a deep crimson as the sun sank behind the hills.
Why do the acacia leaves glow such a rich crimson at dusk?
"Look at that crimson horizon," Samira whispered, pointing across the savannah.
I painted the sunset in bold crimson to capture that unforgettable evening.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - r - i - m - s - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'crimson', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The area around a particular place.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: There are many different bird species in the vicinity of the lake.
practiceSentences: There are many different bird species in the vicinity of the lake.
Many colourful bird species live in the vicinity of the wide, shining lake.
Are there any watering holes in the vicinity of our new campsite?
"Stay in the vicinity of the tents," the ranger warned as darkness fell.
I spotted three shy antelope grazing quietly in the vicinity of the river.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: v - i - c - i - n - i - t - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'vicinity', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Done by or belonging to a whole group acting together.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The silence of the villagers was a collective act of quiet resistance.
practiceSentences: The silence of the villagers was a collective act of quiet resistance.
The villagers' silence became a powerful collective act of peaceful resistance.
How can a collective effort achieve what no single person could manage alone?
"Our collective voice matters," the elder reminded the determined crowd.
I believe collective kindness can transform an entire struggling community.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - l - l - e - c - t - i - v - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'collective', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A large group of people gathered together.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: A crowd gathered beside the river to cheer for the old farmer.
practiceSentences: A crowd gathered beside the river to cheer for the old farmer.
A large crowd gathered beside the river to cheer for the old farmer.
How did such a peaceful crowd stay so calm despite the tense situation?
"Keep the crowd together," the elder urged as the soldiers approached.
I felt the energy of the cheering crowd fill the entire stadium.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - r - o - w - d
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'crowd', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To make sure that something happens or is done.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Rangers work hard to ensure that wild animals are protected.
practiceSentences: Rangers work hard to ensure that wild animals are protected.
The rangers work tirelessly to ensure that the wild animals stay protected.
What steps can we take to ensure the drinking water remains clean and safe?
"Ensure everyone has eaten," the elder instructed before the long journey began.
I double-check my work to ensure I have not made any careless mistakes.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - n - s - u - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'ensure', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Causing or involving a lot of physical force or harm.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The villagers chose peaceful resistance instead of violent actions.
practiceSentences: The villagers chose peaceful resistance instead of violent actions.
The villagers chose peaceful protest instead of any violent or harmful action.
Why does a violent response so often make a difficult situation far worse?
"Avoid violent words," the elder advised, "for they only deepen the quarrel."
I admired how the crowd stayed calm and refused to turn violent.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: v - i - o - l - e - n - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'violent', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Causing worry or upset.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The news about the burning of school books was very disturbing.
practiceSentences: The news about the burning of school books was very disturbing.
The disturbing news about the burned schoolbooks spread quickly through the frightened village.
Why did the strange silence in the forest feel so deeply disturbing?
"That is a disturbing rumour," the elder said, frowning at the messenger.
I found the film's cruelty disturbing, so I switched it off halfway through.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - i - s - t - u - r - b - i - n - g
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'disturbing', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Related to the study of ancient cultures through their buildings, tools, and objects.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The archaeological site near the village revealed tools from thousands of years ago.
practiceSentences: The archaeological site near the village revealed tools from thousands of years ago.
The archaeological dig near the village revealed tools thousands of years old.
What archaeological treasures might still lie buried beneath these ancient fields?
"This is an important archaeological site," the expert told the fascinated pupils.
I would love to join an archaeological team and uncover forgotten history.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - r - c - h - a - e - o - l - o - g - i - c - a - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'archaeological', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Life in the Wild.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Describe how food chains and ecosystems work, using the correct scientific vocabulary.
Use the present simple tense to state facts about animals and nature.
Build complex sentences using relative clauses with who, which, and where.
Write an animal research report with clear paragraphs and accurate information.
Discuss how humans affect wildlife and what can be done to protect endangered species.
Identify and use key vocabulary related to the natural world: habitat, predator, prey, species, ecosystem, and more.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Read, understand, and respond to a text about wildlife and survival.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Speak confidently about the natural world and present your findings to others.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: Life in the Wild: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Unit 3: Life in the Wild! In this unit, you will explore the natural world through the lens of East African ecosystems. You will discover how animals, plants, and their environments are connected in amazing ways. Every living thing depends on something else to survive, and understanding these connections helps us take better care of the world around us.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Describe how food chains and ecosystems work, using the correct scientific vocabulary.
Use the present simple tense to state facts about animals and nature.
Build complex sentences using relative clauses with who, which, and where.
Write an animal research report with clear paragraphs and accurate information.
Discuss how humans affect wildlife and what can be done to protect endangered species.
Identify and use key vocabulary related to the natural world: habitat, predator, prey, species, ecosystem, and more.
Read, understand, and respond to a text about wildlife and survival.
Speak confidently about the natural world and present your findings to others.
Before you begin reading, make sure you understand these important words. You will meet them again and again throughout the unit. Study the full definitions in your Vocabulary companion file.
ecosystem – a community of living things and their environment, all connected together
habitat – the natural home of an animal or plant
food chain – a series showing which creature eats which
predator – an animal that hunts other animals for food
prey – an animal that is hunted by another animal
species – a group of living things that are the same kind
amphibian – an animal that lives both in water and on land
carbon – a basic element found in all living things
observable – something that can be seen or noticed
crimson – a deep red colour
vicinity – the area around a particular place
endure – to keep going through something difficult without giving up
threat – something that can cause harm or danger
treacherous – dangerous and not safe, often not what it seems
starvation – the condition of not having enough food
Reading: The Web of Life in East Africa
Read the following passage carefully. As you read, pay attention to the present simple tense used to state facts, and notice the relative clauses that add extra information about animals and places. The words in bold are from your vocabulary list.
East Africa is a region which stretches from the dry plains of Somalia to the green highlands of Ethiopia and the vast savannahs of Kenya and Tanzania. It is home to one of the richest collections of wildlife on Earth. Every animal, plant, and insect plays a part in the great web of life. Scientists call this web an ecosystem — a community of living things that depend on each other and on the land, water, and air around them.

readings.title: Life in the Wild: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
At the heart of every ecosystem lies the food chain. A food chain shows who eats whom. It begins with plants, which use sunlight and carbon dioxide to make their own food through a process called photosynthesis. These plants are called producers because they produce energy for the entire chain. In the semi-arid grasslands of the Horn of Africa, tough grasses and thorny acacia trees are the main producers. They endure months of dry heat and burst into life when the rains finally come.
The next link in the chain is the herbivore — an animal that eats only plants. On the East African savannah, herds of zebra, wildebeest, and gazelle graze on the grasslands. In the drier regions near Somali towns, the camel — which people sometimes call the “ship of the desert” — feeds on dry shrubs and thorny branches that other animals cannot reach. The gerenuk, a slender antelope which is found in Somalia and Kenya, stands on its hind legs to browse the highest leaves. Each herbivore has its own habitat and its own way of surviving.
Then come the predators — the meat-eaters who hunt other animals for food. The lion, which lives in family groups called prides, is one of East Africa’s most famous predators. Cheetahs, who are the fastest land animals on Earth, chase gazelle across the open plains. The Somali wild dog hunts in packs, working together to catch prey that is larger than any single dog. High above, eagles and vultures circle the sky, watching for small mammals and reptiles below. These birds of prey play an important role because they clean the environment by eating dead animals.
Water creates its own chain. Along the rivers and seasonal pools, frogs and toads are common. These amphibians — animals that live both in water and on land — eat insects and are in turn eaten by snakes and herons. Off the coast of East Africa, the coral reefs are underwater ecosystems where thousands of species of fish, crabs, and sea turtles find food and shelter. Whale sharks, which are the largest fish in the ocean, visit these warm waters to feed on tiny plankton.
Every part of this web matters. Remove one species, and the whole chain can fall apart. When farmers clear land for crops, they destroy the habitat of wild animals. When hunters kill too many predators, the number of herbivores grows too large, and they eat all the grass until the land turns to dust. When plastic waste reaches the ocean, it becomes a threat to sea turtles who mistake it for jellyfish. The balance of nature is treacherous — it can look strong, but it is easily broken.

readings.title: The Red Cloth, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
The sun rose slowly over the hills of Kalembo, a village surrounded by dust and silence. Three months ago, everything had changed.
Foreign soldiers had arrived. They wore sharp grey coats and marched in tight rows. Without warning, they had proclaimed that Kalembo now belonged to a land “across the sea.” No one had seen this ruler. People called him a dictator, someone who gave orders no one could question.
The soldiers took control of the grain stores. They set up new laws in an administration centre — a stone building filled with papers and stamps. “This is what they call bureaucracy,” said Bibi Sophia, the village elder. “A system where rules matter more than people.”
Twelve-year-old Sophia had lived in Kalembo her whole life. She remembered laughter, music, and baskets of mangoes under the fig tree. Now, she heard orders barked in a language she didn’t understand. People whispered more than they spoke. Soldiers watched every corner.
One day, a funeral was held for Mzee Idris, an old farmer who had refused to hand over his harvest. He had died quietly in his sleep, some said from starvation.
A crowd gathered beside the river to mourn and bury him. Women wore shawls. Men stood in silence. Children held hands.
Suddenly, a group of soldiers arrived.
“No gatherings allowed!” one of them shouted. “This is a prohibited event.”
“But it’s a funeral,” someone said.
“All events must be approved,” said the soldier. “Even grief must follow rules.”
The villagers dispersed slowly, heads bowed. But something stayed — an emotion deeper than sorrow. Frustration.
Nadia stood beside her mother and whispered, “They fear even our tears.”
That night, Sophia asked her grandfather, “Why do they treat us this way?”
“Because people who oppress others are afraid,” he said. “They fear what they cannot control — memory, belief, unity.”
She didn’t fully understand, but she remembered his words.
The next day, the soldiers painted warnings on walls:
“Any acts of resistance will be punished. Anyone who refuses to obey will be treated as treacherous.”
The words were meant to scare. But they lit a fire in Sophia’s heart.
“These words,” she told her friend Isaac, “are like weapons.”
“Then we need words too,” he said.
That night, they gathered red cloth scraps — bright, crimson, the colour Mzee Idris had worn when he walked through town. Sophia’s mother had once called it “a colour no fear can hide.”
Nadia, Isaac, and others tied the cloths on trees, water pots, rooftops. On fences, they painted messages in charcoal:
They didn’t sign the messages. But the whole vicinity began to feel different. A wind of quiet courage blew through the village.

readings.title: The Red Cloth, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
When the soldiers saw the red cloths, they grew angry.
“Who’s behind this?” they shouted. “These symbols are treacherous.”
But no one spoke. It was a collective silence. The villagers had found power in not revealing the truth.
The next day, all the cloths were torn down. But by sunset, new ones had appeared.
At school, the teacher gave a new writing assignment:
“Choose someone who showed courage. Write how they faced hardship, and how they responded.”
Nadia wrote about her grandmother, who had once hidden refugees fleeing the war. “She was quiet,” Sophia wrote, “but she acted. That is strength.”
Isaac wrote about a man named Isaac, an abolitionist who had fought slavery through song and secret messages.
Others wrote about leaders from the past. One wrote:
“A dictator builds walls. But people build bridges.”
“My grandmother, who raised me, believed in truth.”
“Isaac, whose words became songs, never gave up.”
“A soldier is someone who follows orders, which can destroy or protect.”
One morning, the soldiers arrived at school.
“These books,” they said, “contain propaganda.”
They burned several. “You must only learn what we allow.”
Nadia looked down, but something inside her refused to shrink.
That night, she and others replaced the school gate sign with a painted message:
“Knowledge is not treason. Burning pages doesn’t erase truth.”
That week, there were whispers of sickness. A health worker arrived from the capital to test the water. “This is a case of typhoid,” he said. “Caused by unsafe drinking water.”
He handed out carbon filters and gave instructions. “Boil water. Avoid contaminated streams. Illness is not always observable. It hides.”
Later she told Isaac, “Injustice is like typhoid. You don’t always see it, but it poisons everything.”
One day, the red cloths disappeared.
The villagers looked confused. No one had removed them. No one had put up new ones.
That afternoon, a new rule was posted:
“You may bury your dead in groups of five or fewer. All funerals must end before sunset.”
“It’s not freedom,” someone muttered. “But it’s a crack in the wall.”
Nadia’s grandfather said, “It shows they feel pressure. They see we endure.”
The teacher asked the class to reflect:
“What happens when people face power they cannot fight?”
Nadia raised her hand. “Some plant roots. Quiet ones. Ones that reach deep.”
As the months passed, small changes grew.
The soldiers patrolled less. They shouted less. The new bureaucrats were local hires. The cloths returned, not just as symbols, but in clothes, art, and flags.
One day, years later, a traveller came to Kalembo. He had heard of the red cloths. He asked the villagers:

grammar.title: The Present Simple Tense — Stating Facts
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
The present simple tense is used to talk about facts, habits, and things that are always or generally true. When we write about science, nature, or the world around us, we use this tense to state facts. For example, 'The Earth orbits the Sun' is a fact that is always true, so we use the present simple.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
The present simple tense is used to talk about facts, habits, and things that are always or generally true. When we write about science, nature, or the world around us, we use this tense to state facts. For example, 'The Earth orbits the Sun' is a fact that is always true, so we use the present simple.
This is important for Unit 3 because when you write about animals, ecosystems, and food chains, you need to state facts clearly and accurately.
How to form it
The present simple is formed using the base form of the verb. For he, she, and it, add -s or -es to the verb.
I / you / we / they + base verb:  "Lions hunt in groups."
He / she / it + verb + s/es:  "A cheetah runs very fast."
Special spelling rules for he/she/it:
Most verbs: add -s (eat → eats, live → lives, play → plays)
Verbs ending in -s, -sh, -ch, -x, -o: add -es (watch → watches, go → goes, catch → catches)
Verbs ending in consonant + y: change y to i, add -es (fly → flies, carry → carries)
Irregular: have → has, do → does, be → is
Negative form
To make a negative, use do not (don't) or does not (doesn't) + base verb:
"Herbivores do not eat meat."
"A frog does not live only in water."
"Camels don't need much water."
Question form
To ask a question, use Do or Does + subject + base verb:
"Do lions hunt at night?"
"Does the whale shark eat fish?"
"Do amphibians live on land and in water?"
Examples using wildlife facts
"The African elephant is the largest land animal."
"Cheetahs run faster than any other land animal."
"A food chain shows which creature eats which."
"Plants produce energy through photosynthesis."
"The Nile crocodile lives in rivers and lakes across East Africa."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake 1: Forgetting the -s for he/she/it.
"The eagle fly high." ✗  →  "The eagle flies high." ✓
Mistake 2: Using the present continuous instead of the present simple for facts.
"A camel is storing fat in its humps." ✗  →  "A camel stores fat in its humps." ✓
Mistake 3: Adding -s with do/does in negatives or questions.
"Does the lion hunts at night?" ✗  →  "Does the lion hunt at night?" ✓
Memory tip
Think: Fact = Present Simple. If it is always true — right now, yesterday, and tomorrow — use the present simple. Ask yourself: "Is this a fact about the world?" If yes, keep the verb simple!
Practice 1
Choose the correct form of the verb in brackets.
A vulture ______ (clean / cleans) the environment by eating dead animals.
Frogs ______ (live / lives) both in water and on land.
The gerenuk ______ (stand / stands) on its hind legs to eat from tall bushes.
Plants ______ (use / uses) sunlight to make food.
An ecosystem ______ (include / includes) all the living things in one area.
Herbivores ______ (do not eat / does not eat) meat.
______ (Do / Does) the whale shark hunt large fish?
The camel ______ (store / stores) fat in its humps.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply the present simple tense — stating facts in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Present Simple — The Verb 'to be' for Facts
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
The verb to be (am, is, are) is one of the most important verbs in English. In the present simple, we use it to describe what something is, to classify things, and to state facts about identity and characteristics.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
The verb to be (am, is, are) is one of the most important verbs in English. In the present simple, we use it to describe what something is, to classify things, and to state facts about identity and characteristics.
How to form it
I → am:  "I am a student."
He / she / it → is:  "The cheetah is the fastest land animal."
You / we / they → are:  "Coral reefs are underwater ecosystems."
Negative: add not (or use contractions: isn't, aren't):
"A whale shark is not (isn't) a whale. It is a fish."
"Camels are not (aren't) predators."
Questions: put the verb before the subject:
"Is the gerenuk an antelope?"
"Are amphibians cold-blooded?"
Examples using wildlife facts
"The Somali wild ass is one of the most endangered animals in the world."
"Frogs are amphibians."
"The savannah is a grassland where many animals live."
"Coral reefs are home to thousands of species."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: Using "is" with a plural subject.
"The elephants is large." ✗  →  "The elephants are large." ✓
Mistake: Using "are" with a singular subject.
"The lion are a predator." ✗  →  "The lion is a predator." ✓
Practice 2
Fill in the blank with is, are, isn't, or aren't.
The African elephant ______ the largest land animal.
Frogs ______ mammals. They ______ amphibians.
A food chain ______ a series showing which creature eats which.
Vultures ______ predators that hunt live animals. They eat dead ones.
The Serengeti ______ a place where the great migration takes place.
______ the whale shark a dangerous animal? No, it ______.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply present simple — the verb 'to be' for facts in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Relative Clauses with 'who' (for people)
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
A relative clause is a group of words that gives extra information about a noun in a sentence. We use who when we are talking about people. A relative clause with "who" tells us more about a person — what they do, what they believe, or what they are like.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
A relative clause is a group of words that gives extra information about a noun in a sentence. We use who when we are talking about people. A relative clause with "who" tells us more about a person — what they do, what they believe, or what they are like.
Using relative clauses makes your writing more detailed and interesting. Instead of writing two short sentences, you can combine them into one complex sentence.
How to form it
Start with a sentence about a person. Then add a clause beginning with who to give more information:
"Scientists study ecosystems." + "Scientists are called ecologists."
→ "Scientists who study ecosystems are called ecologists."
More examples:
"The ranger who protects the park is called Samira."
"Farmers who clear forests destroy animal habitats."
"The abolitionist who fought slavery used songs and secret messages."
"People who oppress others are afraid of what they cannot control."
Defining vs. non-defining
A defining relative clause tells us exactly which person we mean. It does not use commas:
"The boy who won the race is my friend." (Which boy? The one who won.)
A non-defining relative clause adds extra information. It uses commas:
"Sophia, who was only twelve, showed great courage." (We already know which Sophia.)
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: Using "which" for people.
"The teacher which helped us is kind." ✗  →  "The teacher who helped us is kind." ✓
Mistake: Forgetting commas in non-defining clauses.
"Sophia who was twelve showed courage." ✗  →  "Sophia, who was twelve, showed courage." ✓
Memory tip
Who = people. If you can replace "who" with a person's name, you are using it correctly.
Practice 3
Combine each pair of sentences into one, using who.
The ranger protects wild animals. She works in a national park.
Scientists discovered a new species of frog. They were studying the cloud forest.
The farmer refused to hand over his harvest. He died from starvation.
Nadia showed great courage. She was only twelve years old.
An abolitionist fights against slavery. He or she believes all people should be free.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply relative clauses with 'who' (for people) in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Relative Clauses with 'which' and 'that' (for animals and things)
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
We use which when we are talking about animals, things, and ideas — never for people. We can also use that for animals and things, especially in defining clauses. Using "which" and "that" lets you add important details to your sentences about the natural world.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
We use which when we are talking about animals, things, and ideas — never for people. We can also use that for animals and things, especially in defining clauses. Using "which" and "that" lets you add important details to your sentences about the natural world.
How to form it
Start with a sentence about an animal or thing. Add a clause beginning with which or that:
"The coral reef provides shelter." + "The coral reef is underwater."
→ "The coral reef, which is underwater, provides shelter."
More examples:
"The camel, which can survive without water for days, is found across East Africa."
"A food chain is a series that shows which creature eats which."
"The lion, which lives in prides, is a famous predator."
"An ecosystem that loses its top predator can fall apart."
When to use 'that' vs. 'which'
That is used in defining clauses (no commas) — it tells us exactly which thing we mean:
"The animal that eats only plants is called a herbivore."
Which can be used in both defining and non-defining clauses. Non-defining clauses (with commas) add extra, non-essential information:
"The elephant, which is the largest land animal, lives in family groups."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: Using "who" for animals or things.
"The tree who grows near the river is an acacia." ✗  →  "The tree which grows near the river is an acacia." ✓
Mistake: Forgetting to include the relative pronoun.
"The animal eats plants is a herbivore." ✗  →  "The animal that eats plants is a herbivore." ✓
Memory tip
Which = things and animals. If you are talking about a creature with four legs, wings, fins, or roots in the ground, use "which" or "that" — never "who".
Practice 4
Fill in each blank with which or that. Add commas where needed for non-defining clauses.
The cheetah is a predator ______ runs faster than any other land animal.
Coral reefs ______ are found off the East African coast support thousands of species.
The gerenuk ______ stands on its hind legs is found in Somalia and Kenya.
An ecosystem ______ loses its key species can collapse.
The Serengeti ______ is located in Tanzania sees a massive migration every year.
A food chain is a diagram ______ shows the flow of energy from one creature to another.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply relative clauses with 'which' and 'that' (for animals and things) in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Relative Clauses with 'where' (for places)
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
We use where when we are talking about places. A relative clause with "where" tells us more about a location — what happens there, who lives there, or why it is important. This is especially useful when describing habitats and ecosystems.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
We use where when we are talking about places. A relative clause with "where" tells us more about a location — what happens there, who lives there, or why it is important. This is especially useful when describing habitats and ecosystems.
How to form it
Start with a sentence about a place. Add a clause beginning with where:
"The savannah is a grassland." + "Many animals graze on the savannah."
→ "The savannah is a grassland where many animals graze."
More examples:
"Coral reefs are underwater ecosystems where thousands of species find food and shelter."
"The national park is a protected area where no hunting is allowed."
"The river is a place where frogs, fish, and herons live side by side."
"East Africa is a region where some of the world's most incredible wildlife can be found."
Common mistakes and how to fix them
Mistake: Using "which" instead of "where" for a place when it acts as a location.
"The park which we play" ✗  →  "The park where we play" ✓
Note: you can use "which" for a place when it is the subject or object of the clause:
"The park, which is very large, has many animals." ✓ ("which" = the park = subject)
But when you mean "in that place", use where:
"The park where many animals live is protected." ✓ ("where" = in the park)
Memory tip
Where = places. If you can replace "where" with "in that place" or "at that place", you are using it correctly.
Practice 5
Complete each sentence with where and your own ending.
The Serengeti is a grassland where ______.
The coral reef is an underwater habitat where ______.
The forest is a place where ______.
The river is a habitat where ______.
East Africa is a region where ______.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply relative clauses with 'where' (for places) in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Combining Present Simple with Relative Clauses
grammar.explanation:
What it means and why it matters
The real power of your grammar comes when you combine the present simple tense with relative clauses. This lets you write rich, detailed sentences about the natural world — exactly what you need for your animal research report and your Cambridge Primary English assessments.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What it means and why it matters
The real power of your grammar comes when you combine the present simple tense with relative clauses. This lets you write rich, detailed sentences about the natural world — exactly what you need for your animal research report and your Cambridge Primary English assessments.
How to combine them
Use the present simple for the main fact, and add a relative clause for extra detail:
"The cheetah, which is the fastest land animal, hunts gazelles on the open plains."
"Ecologists are scientists who study how living things interact with their environment."
"The savannah is a grassland where zebras, wildebeest, and gazelles graze."
"A predator is an animal that hunts other animals for food."
Building complex sentences step by step
Start with a simple fact:
"The eagle hunts small mammals."
Add a relative clause for more detail:
"The eagle, which has excellent eyesight, hunts small mammals."
Add a location clause:
"The eagle, which has excellent eyesight, hunts small mammals in the savannah where tall grasses provide cover."
Practice 6
Write five complex sentences about East African wildlife. Each sentence must:
Use the present simple tense for the main verb.
Include at least one relative clause (with who, which, that, or where).
Be factually accurate (ask your AI tutor if you are unsure about a fact).
Here are some animals and places to write about:
The African elephant
The Nile crocodile
The Somali ostrich
The coral reefs of East Africa
The Masai Mara grasslands
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply combining present simple with relative clauses in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

writing.title: Writing 1: Animal Research Report
writing.promptAndInstructions:
In this section, you will plan and write a short research report about an East African animal. This is a key skill for your Cambridge Primary English course. A good report presents facts clearly and uses the present simple tense throughout.
Step 1: Choose Your Animal
Pick one animal from the list below, or choose your own East African animal:
The Somali wild ass
The gerenuk
The whale shark
The African elephant
The Nile crocodile
The East African crowned crane
The cheetah
The Somali ostrich
Step 2: Research Your Animal
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 6 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about animal research report.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with evidence; considered viewpoint where relevant; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a clear structure, topic sentences and supporting evidence, then use the word bank and source models.
writing.extension: Synthesize details from more than one text, vary sentence structures, improve transitions and strengthen the conclusion.

activities.title: Activities 1: Food Chain Diagram
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Draw a food chain for the East African savannah. Include at least four links. Label each one as a producer, herbivore, or predator. Then write one sentence about each link using the present simple tense and a relative clause. For example:
“The acacia tree is a producer which makes food using sunlight.”
“The gazelle is a herbivore that eats acacia leaves.”
“The cheetah is a predator which hunts gazelles.”
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2: True or False
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False. If the statement is false, correct it.
A predator is an animal that eats only plants.
An amphibian lives both in water and on land.
The present simple tense is used for facts that are always true.
We use “who” for things and “which” for people.
The coral reef is an underwater ecosystem.
Camels are predators that hunt other animals.
Vultures help clean the environment.
A relative clause gives extra information about a verb.
Answers: 1. False (a predator hunts other animals)  2. True  3. True  4. False (who for people, which for things)  5. True  6. False (camels are herbivores)  7. True  8. False (a relative clause gives extra information about a noun)
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3: Vocabulary Crossword Clues
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write the correct vocabulary word for each clue.
A community of living things and their environment: e________
The natural home of an animal or plant: h________
An animal that hunts other animals: p________
An animal that is hunted: p________
A group of living things of the same kind: s________
A deep red colour: c________
To keep going through difficulty: e________
The area around a particular place: v________
Answers: 1. ecosystem  2. habitat  3. predator  4. prey  5. species  6. crimson  7. endure  8. vicinity
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4: Critical Thinking — Predicting Animal Survival
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Read each situation and predict what might happen. Explain your reasoning using because and the present simple tense.
All the lions in an area disappear. What happens to the zebra population? What happens to the grass?
A factory pollutes the river near a village. What happens to the frogs and fish? What happens to the animals that eat them?
Climate change causes the rains to come later each year. How does this affect the plants? How does it affect the herbivores?
A community decides to create a protected area where no hunting is allowed. What changes might you see after five years?
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5: Compare and Contrast
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Choose two East African animals (for example, the cheetah and the Nile crocodile). Write a short paragraph comparing them. Use these sentence starters:
"Both the ______ and the ______ are predators which ______."
"The ______, which lives in ______, hunts by ______."
"However, the ______ ______, while the ______ ______."
"Both animals are important to the ecosystem because ______."
Your AI tutor is ready to help you 24 hours a day. Use it well:
Ask for another explanation: “I don’t understand ecosystems. Can you explain it in a different way?”
Practise speaking: “Can you listen to my animal presentation and tell me how to improve?”
Get feedback: “Can you check my research report and tell me what to fix?”
Ask for more practice: “Can you give me more exercises on relative clauses?”
Prepare for your teacher: “What should I be ready to show my teacher this week?”
Explore further: “Tell me more about endangered animals in East Africa.”
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6: Vocabulary Sort and Explain

activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort twelve unit words into useful categories. Explain the rule for each category in a complete sentence.

quizzes.question: What does 'threat' mean?
quizzes.options: A difficult situation that causes suffering or trouble. | A leader who has complete power and makes all the rules without asking others. | Something that can cause harm or danger. | To treat someone in a cruel or unfair way, making them feel weak or powerless.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Something that can cause harm or danger.
quizzes.explanation: The dark clouds are a threat of a coming storm.

quizzes.question: What does 'treacherous' mean?
quizzes.options: A difficult situation that causes suffering or trouble. | Dangerous and not safe, often because it is not what it seems. | To treat someone badly or unfairly because of their beliefs or identity. | To treat someone in a cruel or unfair way, making them feel weak or powerless.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Dangerous and not safe, often because it is not what it seems.
quizzes.explanation: The path through the mountains was treacherous after the rains.

quizzes.question: What does 'dictator' mean?
quizzes.options: A difficult situation that causes suffering or trouble. | A leader who has complete power and makes all the rules without asking others. | To say that something is not allowed. | To treat someone badly or unfairly because of their beliefs or identity.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A leader who has complete power and makes all the rules without asking others.
quizzes.explanation: The dictator did not allow people to speak freely or choose their leaders.

quizzes.question: What does 'oppress' mean?
quizzes.options: To keep going through something difficult without giving up. | To say that something is not allowed. | To treat someone badly or unfairly because of their beliefs or identity. | To treat someone in a cruel or unfair way, making them feel weak or powerless.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To treat someone in a cruel or unfair way, making them feel weak or powerless.
quizzes.explanation: The soldiers tried to oppress the villagers by taking away their rights.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Life in the Wild: source text 2': When plastic waste reaches the ocean, it becomes a _____ to sea turtles who mistake it for jellyfish.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: When plastic waste reaches the ocean, it becomes a threat to sea turtles who mistake it for jellyfish.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Life in the Wild: source text 2': The balance of nature is _____ — it can look strong, but it is easily broken.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: The balance of nature is treacherous — it can look strong, but it is easily broken.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Red Cloth, part 1': People called him a _____, someone who gave orders no one could question.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: People called him a dictator, someone who gave orders no one could question.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Red Cloth, part 2': Write how they faced _____, and how they responded.” Nadia wrote about her grandmother, who had once hidden refugees fleeing the war.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Write how they faced hardship, and how they responded.” Nadia wrote about her grandmother, who had once hidden refugees fleeing the war.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Life in the Wild: source text 2': They _____ months of dry heat and burst into life when the rains finally come.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: They endure months of dry heat and burst into life when the rains finally come.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Red Cloth, part 1': Without warning, they had _____ that Kalembo now belonged to a land “across the sea.” No one had seen this ruler.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Without warning, they had proclaimed that Kalembo now belonged to a land “across the sea.” No one had seen this ruler.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you explain what a food chain is using an example from my country?
What is the difference between a predator and prey?
Can you help me draw a simple food chain for the East African savannah?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you give me five sentences about East African animals that use relative clauses?
Can you check if I am using who, which, and where correctly?
What is the difference between a defining and non-defining relative clause?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you check my animal report for grammar mistakes?
Can you help me add more relative clauses to my writing?
Can you suggest a better opening sentence for my report?
Can you quiz me on the vocabulary I used in my report?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you listen to my animal presentation and give me feedback?
Can you play the Food Chain Challenge with me?
Can you help me improve my pronunciation of the vocabulary words?
Can you ask me questions about the wildlife documentary to test my listening?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you quiz me on the power and control words?
Can you help me use these words in sentences about real situations?
What is the difference between a dictator and a fair leader?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you quiz me on the community and society words?
Can you explain what an abolitionist does using real historical examples?
Can you help me write a paragraph using at least four words from this group?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you quiz me on the nature and environment words?
Can you help me understand how carbon moves through an ecosystem?
Can you explain what typhoid is and how it spreads?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you quiz me on the group words?
Can you help me understand the difference between ensure and endure?
Can you give me example sentences using collective and crowd?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you check my paragraph and tell me if I used the words correctly?
Can you listen to my story and give me feedback?
Can you help me find more connections between the vocabulary words?
Can you give me a vocabulary test on all the Unit 3 words?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Can you check my grammar answers and explain any I got wrong?
Can you give me five more sentences to practise relative clauses?
What is the difference between a defining and non-defining relative clause?
Can you help me combine short sentences using who, which, and where?

answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Something that can cause harm or danger. The dark clouds are a threat of a coming storm.
Dangerous and not safe, often because it is not what it seems. The path through the mountains was treacherous after the rains.
A leader who has complete power and makes all the rules without asking others. The dictator did not allow people to speak freely or choose their leaders.
To treat someone in a cruel or unfair way, making them feel weak or powerless. The soldiers tried to oppress the villagers by taking away their rights.
A difficult situation that causes suffering or trouble. After the drought, many families faced hardship because they lost their crops.
To treat someone badly or unfairly because of their beliefs or identity. It is wrong to persecute people just because they are different.
To say that something is not allowed. The soldiers tried to prohibit gatherings in the village.
To keep going through something difficult without giving up. The villagers had to endure months of harsh rules and limited food.
To say something loudly or officially. The leader proclaimed that the village now belonged to a foreign power.
A ceremony to remember and say goodbye to someone who has died. The whole community gathered at the funeral to honour the old farmer.
